invariant_definition: Śuddhādvaita is the Vedānta school of Vallabha (15th–16th c.) and his Puṣṭi-Mārga tradition of Kṛṣṇa devotion. It is "pure" non-duality in that it affirms the world and souls as real manifestations of Brahman (identified with Kṛṣṇa) without admitting māyā as an illusion-producing power — distinguishing it from Śaṅkara's Advaita. Brahman undergoes an unmodified self-manifestation (avikṛta-pariṇāma): it becomes the effect-form while remaining wholly itself, as gold remains gold in its ornaments. The jīva and the world are real parts (aṃśa) of Brahman; bondage is the suppression of the soul's ānanda aspect, and liberation comes through divine grace (puṣṭi) and devotion to Kṛṣṇa.
